title: What is a function
instruction:
Think about a library. You walk up to the counter and ask the librarian for a book. You do not need to know how they find it — you just ask and they bring it.

A function is the same idea. You give a chunk of code a name. Then you just call that name — and everything inside runs.

Run this. The `sayHello` function is defined above main and called from inside it.
successMessage: Three calls, one definition. Change the message inside sayHello and all three lines change. That is the point of functions.

title: Define and call your own function
hint: func printDivider() { fmt.Println(\"----------\") }  — put it above func main(), then call printDivider() three times inside main.

codeChecks.message: Define the function: func printDivider() { ... }
Call printDivider() three times inside main.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your function, your name, your call. That is all there is to defining and using a function.

title: Parameters — give the function inputs
instruction:
A function with no parameters always does exactly the same thing. More useful is a function that accepts inputs and does something with them.

`func greet(name string)` — this function takes one string and uses it.

Change the name passed to `greet` to your own name and run it.
hint: Change \"Alex\" to your name in the greet() call inside main.

codeChecks.message: Change the name from \"Alex\" to your own name.

successMessage: Parameters let you pass information into a function. Same function, different input, different result.

title: Multiple parameters
instruction:
Functions can take more than one parameter. Separate them with commas.

`func add(a int, b int) int` — takes two ints.

If two parameters have the same type, you can shorten it: `func add(a, b int)`.

Add a call to `describe` with your own name and age, then run it.
hint: describe(\"Alex\", 25)  — replace Alex and 25 with your own name and age.

codeChecks.message: Call describe with a name and age: describe(\"YourName\", yourAge)
Replace the TODO comment with your call.

successMessage: Multiple parameters, one function. Order matters — the values you pass must match the parameter order.

title: Return a value
instruction:
Functions can give something back using `return`.

You declare the return type after the parameter list:

`func double(n int) int { return n * 2 }`

The `int` after the closing parenthesis is the return type.

Call `countBooks` with three different shelf sizes and print each result.

codeChecks.message: Call countBooks(4) and print the result.
Call countBooks(7) and print the result.
Call countBooks(10) and print the result.
Replace the TODO comment with your three calls.

successMessage: Return sends a value back to wherever the function was called. You can use it directly in Println, assign it to a variable, or pass it to another function.

title: Multiple return values
instruction:
Go can return more than one value from a function. This is one of Go's most useful features.

`func minMax(a, b int) (int, int)` — returns two ints.

This is how Go handles errors too: `result, err := doSomething()`.

Call `findBook` and print both the section number and the shelf number.
hint: section, shelf := findBook("Go", 5)  then fmt.Println(section, shelf)

codeChecks.message: Call findBook(\"Go\", 5)
Capture both return values: section, shelf := findBook(\"Go\", 5)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Section 1, shelf 2. Two values, one return. This pattern is everywhere in Go — especially for error handling.

title: Named return values
instruction:
You can give return values names. This makes it clear what each one represents.

```
func stats(nums []int) (min, max int) {
    min = nums[0]
    max = nums[0]
    // ...
    return  // naked return — returns min and max
}
```

The named variables are pre-declared. A bare `return` sends them back.

Run this and see what it returns for the given shelf.
successMessage: Named returns act like pre-declared variables inside the function. The naked return sends them back. Use named returns when the names make the code clearer.

title: Variadic functions
instruction:
A variadic function accepts any number of arguments.

`func sum(nums ...int)` — the `...` means zero or more ints.

Inside the function, `nums` is a slice. You can loop it, pass it around, use `len()` on it.

Call `checkMember` with different sets of book IDs and print each result.

codeChecks.message: Call checkMember(101, 102, 103)
Call checkMember(201, 202)
Call checkMember(301)
Replace the TODO comment with your calls.

successMessage: Any number of book IDs. fmt.Println itself is variadic — that is how it handles fmt.Println(a, b, c) without complaining.

title: Functions as values
instruction:
In Go, functions are values. You can assign them to variables, store them in maps, pass them to other functions.

`var double func(int) int = func(n int) int { return n * 2 }`

Or shorter: `double := func(n int) int { return n * 2 }`

Run this. Then add your own `triple` function variable that multiplies by 3 and call it.
hint: triple := func(n int) int { return n * 3 }  then fmt.Println(triple(5))

codeChecks.message: Create a triple function variable: triple := func(n int) int { return n * 3 }
Call triple(5) and print the result.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Functions are first-class values in Go. You can store them, pass them, return them. This unlocks patterns like callbacks and middleware.

title: Anonymous functions
instruction:
An anonymous function has no name. It is defined and used inline.

Useful when you need a small function once and do not want to name it.

```
result := func(a, b int) int {
    return a + b
}(3, 4)  // called immediately
```

Run this. Then add an anonymous function that counts books on two shelves and prints the result immediately.
hint: fmt.Println(func(a, b int) int { return a * b }(6, 7))

codeChecks.message: Define and immediately call an anonymous function with (6, 7).
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Define it. Use it. Done. No name needed for one-time-use logic.

title: Closures
instruction:
A closure is a function that remembers variables from the scope where it was created — even after that scope is gone.

The counter below remembers `count` between calls. Each call to `counter()` increments and returns it.

Run it. Then create a second counter and verify the two counters are independent.
hint: c2 := makeCounter()  then call c2() a couple of times and print. It starts at 1 independently.

codeChecks.message: Create a second counter: c2 := makeCounter()
Call c2() and print the result.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: c1 and c2 each have their own count. They share the same function definition but have separate state. That is a closure.

title: Recursion
instruction:
A recursive function calls itself.

It sounds circular, but it works as long as there is a base case — a condition where it stops calling itself.

```
func countdown(n int) {
    if n == 0 { return }  // base case
    fmt.Println(n)
    countdown(n - 1)      // calls itself
}
```

Run this function that calculates shelves needed. Then call it with 6 and print the result.

codeChecks.message: Call shelvesNeeded(6) and print the result.
Replace the TODO comment with your call.

successMessage: 5 shelves = 120. 6 shelves = 720. The function kept calling itself until it hit n == 0, then unwound back up. Recursion is elegant for tree traversal, file systems, and math.

title: defer
instruction:
`defer` schedules a function call to run at the very end of the surrounding function — no matter what happens in between.

Common uses: closing a file, releasing a lock, returning a book to the shelf.

Run this and notice the order of output. Deferred calls run last, in reverse order if there are multiple.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	defer fmt.Println("Library is now closed")
	defer fmt.Println("Returning all checked-out books...")
	fmt.Println("Opening the library...")
	fmt.Println("Processing new arrivals...")
}
successMessage: defer runs after everything else, in reverse order (last deferred = first to run at cleanup). Think of it as a cleanup guarantee — even if the function panics, the deferred call still runs.

title: Build a library toolkit
instruction:
No starter code.

Write four functions:
- `countBooks(shelves int, booksPerShelf int) int` — returns shelves * booksPerShelf
- `findBook(category string, index int) (int, int)` — returns index / 3, index % 3
- `checkMember(ids ...int) int` — sums all the IDs
- `calculateFees(daysLate int, dailyRate float64) float64` — returns float64 of daysLate * dailyRate; return 0 if daysLate is 0

Then call all four with the following values and print each result:
- countBooks(12, 8)
- findBook("Fiction", 7)
- checkMember(401, 502, 603)
- calculateFees(5, 0.50)
hint: Define each function above main. In main: fmt.Println(countBooks(12, 8)) etc.

codeChecks.message: Define a countBooks function.
Define a findBook function.
Define a checkMember function.
Define a calculateFees function.
Call countBooks(12, 8) and print the result.
Call findBook(\"Fiction\", 7) and print the result.
Call checkMember(401, 502, 603) and print the result.
Call calculateFees(5, 0.50) and print the result.

successMessage: Four library functions, one toolkit. countBooks, findBook, checkMember, calculateFees — every serious library system is just functions calling other functions. You now know how to build them all.
